title: Grief Isn't Linear
subtitle: A reading on the actual shape of grief, versus the stages model
excerpt: A reading on how grief actually moves: not through stages in order, but in waves, returns, and surprises. For the person who feels they should be further along in their grief, or who is bewildered by how unpredictable loss turns out to be.
sourceNotes: Written for homemade.education. Draws on current grief research, including the work of George Bonanno on resilience after loss, and criticism of the Kubler-Ross stage model as a sequential prescription.

content.text: A reading on the shape of grief. The five stages model (denial, anger, bargaining, depression, acceptance) is the most widely known account of how grief moves. It is also widely misread. Elisabeth Kubler-Ross originally described these as responses she observed in people facing terminal diagnosis, not a prescription for how anyone should grieve. The sequential interpretation, which implies that grief has a route and that the end point is acceptance, has created a frame many people measure themselves against and find themselves failing.

Modern grief research tells a different story. Most people are more resilient than the stage model predicts. Many never go through long or disabling grief. Those who do tend to recover in time. The path back to normal life is not a tidy march through named stages. It is more like a wave. You move between the loss and the rest of life. Sharp grief comes back, then eases. You manage for a while, then it returns.

The waves do not follow a schedule and do not reliably diminish across time in a straight line. They tend to become less frequent and less overwhelming over months and years, but they continue to arrive. Anniversaries, seasonal cues, unexpected reminders, and life transitions can bring acute grief back in someone who has, by any reasonable measure, adapted to their loss.
The cost of the stage narrative

When people expect grief to move in order and end at acceptance, the actual experience of grief tends to produce two additional problems alongside the loss itself: the sense of failing to grieve correctly, and guilt about not being where the model says they should be. Being two years out from a death and still having days of acute sadness is not a sign of pathological grief. It is how grief behaves.

The idea that grief should be finished is also tied to the wish for things to return to normal. Grief that goes on too long makes other people uneasy. That puts pressure on the grieving person to perform recovery before it has come.

Grief is not a problem to be solved. It is the presence of love with nowhere to go. The question is not when it ends, but how to carry it in a way that allows the rest of life to continue alongside it. That is not a stage to reach. It is a relationship to develop over time.
Written for homemade.education. Draws on current grief research, including the work of George Bonanno, and criticism of the Kubler-Ross stage model as a sequential prescription.
